metadata.title: The AI Speed Paradox: You Ship More Code and Get Slower

beats.narration_text: Hallo — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'The Speed Paradox: Why Developers Using AI Ship More Code but Get Slower' by Aditi Shinde, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. Does AI actually make developers faster? The data says yes and no — simultaneously. 61% more code shipped. 19% slower on completing tasks, for senior engineers. Both numbers from the same studies. The paradox resolves when you understand what AI removed and what it left behind.

props.segment: The AI Speed Paradox: You Ship More Code and Get Slower
props.command: Does AI actually make developers faster?
output: Code shipped: +61% with AI coding tools.
AI removed the keystroke. The responsibility stayed.

beats.narration_text: Here is the divergence. Plot code shipped over time for a team using AI coding tools: the line goes up sharply. Now plot task completion time for the senior engineers on that team: the line also goes up — meaning it takes longer to complete a task, not shorter. Both metrics are measured from the same data. More code, slower delivery. The explanation: AI removed the execution toil — writing boilerplate, looking up syntax. It added verification load — checking whether the probabilistic output is actually correct. For a junior engineer who was slow at writing, the net is a win. For a senior engineer whose value was judgment, not typing speed, the ratio inverts.

animated_deck.description: Two lines from the same origin, both going up over time. Left axis: 'code shipped (lines)' — line rises steeply. Right axis: 'task completion time' — line also rises. Both labeled. Annotation: '+61% code / −19% speed for senior devs'.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Here is the divergence. Plot code shipped over time for a team using AI coding tools: the line goes up sharply. Now plot task completion time for the senior eng

beats.narration_text: Split the work into execution time and verification time. Execution time: writing the code. AI compresses this dramatically — boilerplate that took twenty minutes takes two seconds. Verification time: checking the output is correct, does not introduce bugs, handles edge cases, integrates with the existing system. This is the part AI does not do. For a senior engineer, verification is not a quick scan — it is a deep read, because the stakes of accepting wrong code are high. The net: execution time shrinks, verification time grows. For senior engineers, the verification load exceeds the execution savings.

manim.description: Side-by-side bar chart. Before AI: tall execution bar, short verification bar. After AI: short execution bar (compressed), tall verification bar. Net total shown as flat or slightly taller for senior engineers. Bars labeled.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. AI coding tools make developers ship 61% more code. Senior engineers using them complete tasks 19% slower. The paradox resolves: AI removed execution toil but added verification load. For senior engineers whose value was judgment, not keystrokes, the ratio inverts. And EEG studies show 17-point quiz-score drops in engineers using AI most aggressively — the cognitive engagement required by real problem-solving is being offloaded along with the boilerplate. AI removed the keystroke. The responsibility stayed.

props.artifactTitle: The AI Speed Paradox: You Ship More Code and Get Slower
props.artifactHeading: What AI coding tools actually change
artifactLines: AI removed the keystroke. The responsibility stayed.
EEG studies: 17-point quiz-score drops in heaviest AI users.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Measure your own verification load with AI coding tools. For the next 5 AI-generated code blocks you accept, log how long verification took versus how long writing from scratch would have taken.' Five data points is enough to see your own ratio. The question is not whether AI is fast — it is whether your verification time exceeds your execution savings. That is a personal measurement. Run this experiment, log the five numbers, and find your own breakeven. Liam, in for Bear.
